title: GUIDELINES FOR THE PRODUCTION, PROCESSING, LABELLING AND MARKETING OF ORGANICALLY PRODUCED FOODS

sections.section: GUIDELINES FOR THE PRODUCTION, PROCESSING, LABELLING AND MARKETING OF ORGANICALLY PRODUCED FOODS
sections.summary: Document title and code: Guidelines for the Production, Processing, Labelling and Marketing of Organically Produced Foods (GL 32-1999).

Non-substantive: table of contents listing the guideline's sections and annexes (scope, definitions, labelling, production rules, permitted substances, inspection/certification, imports, and annexes).

Applies to products bearing or intended to bear descriptive labelling referring to organic production methods, including unprocessed plants and livestock products (Annexes 1 and 3) and processed agricultural and livestock products for human consumption derived from them; defines when a product is regarded as bearing organic indications (terms such as 'organic', 'biodynamic', 'biological', 'ecological' or similar), excludes cases where such terms clearly have no connection to production method, and states that genetically engineered/modified organisms and products from them are not accepted under these guidelines.

Section 2 provides the description of organic production principles and a set of definitions used in the guidelines, covering terms such as agricultural product, audit, certification and certification body, competent authority, a provisional definition of genetically engineered/modified organisms and listed techniques, ingredient, inspection, labelling, livestock, marketing, official accreditation/recognition, operator, plant protection product, preparation, production and veterinary drug.

Describes organic production and livestock husbandry principles requiring farm systems that nurture ecosystems, maintain/enhance soil fertility through biological activity and nutrient recycling, control pests/diseases by ecological, biological and mechanical methods, and manage livestock to promote health and welfare while avoiding use of chemical allopathic veterinary drugs including antibiotics.

Provides the definitions used in the guidelines, including agricultural product, audit, certification and certification body, competent authority, a provisional definition of genetically engineered/modified organisms and listed techniques, ingredient, inspection (including production/processing systems for organic food), labelling, livestock, marketing, official accreditation/recognition, operator, plant protection product, preparation, production and veterinary drug.

Sets labelling and claim rules: organic products must comply with the Codex General Standard and may be labelled as organic only if they were produced/imported under the Section 4 production rules, by operators subject to Section 6 inspection, and the label names or codes the official/recognized inspection or certification body; processed products have additional requirements on ingredient origin, prohibited non-agricultural substances/treatments and inspection, while limited derogations (e.g., up to 5% non‑compliant agricultural ingredients) and member‑country options for products with <95% organic ingredients (including suggested front‑panel percentage claims and typographic rules) are permitted pending further review.

General labelling provisions require adherence to the Codex prepackaged foods standard and permit organic claims only when linked to production under Section 4, inspection under Section 6, and identification of the official/recognized certifier, with additional rules for processed products on ingredient origin, prohibited treatments, and limited derogations (e.g., 5% allowance and national options for <95% organic content and conversion labelling).

sections.section: Labelling of products in transition/conversion to organic
sections.summary: Allows products from farms in conversion to be labelled 'transition to organic' only after 12 months of organic production provided labeling and inspection requirements are met, the wording and prominence do not mislead, single-ingredient foods may display the claim on the principal panel, and the label references the official/approved certification body.
States that labelling of non‑retail containers for the specified organic products must meet the requirements set out in Annex 3, paragraph 10.

sections.section: SECTION 4. RULES OF PRODUCTION AND PREPARATION
sections.summary: Requires organic production to meet at least Annex 1 requirements and allows, where those are not effective, use of substances in Annex 2 (Tables 1–2) or nationally approved substances meeting Section 5.1 criteria for plant protection, fertilizers and soil conditioners, similarly requires Annex 1 processing rules for preparation with Annex 2 (Tables 3–4) or approved alternatives, mandates storage/transport per Annex 1, and permits the competent authority to grant derogations or implementation periods for livestock provisions.

sections.section: SECTION 5.                                    REQUIREMENTS FOR INCLUSION OF SUBSTANCES IN ANNEX 2 AND CRITERIA FOR THE DEVELOPMENT OF LISTS OF SUBSTANCES BY COUNTRIES
sections.summary: Establishes criteria for adding substances to Annex 2: proposals must align with organic principles, be necessary for intended use, avoid environmental harm, minimize negative health impacts, and be used only when approved alternatives are unavailable, with additional specific criteria for fertilizers/soil conditioners, pest control agents and processing aids/additives, and requires stakeholder involvement and that countries develop/adopt lists meeting these criteria.

sections.section: The open nature of the lists
sections.summary: Explains that evaluation of permitted substances must be holistic to protect organic integrity and gives specific criteria: fertilization inputs must be essential, of plant/animal/microbial/mineral origin with primarily physical/biological processing and chemical processes as a last resort, must not harm soil or water/air quality and may be restricted regionally; pest control substances must be essential where alternatives are ineffective and pose minimal environmental and health risks, with chemically synthesized pheromones allowable in traps only if natural forms are unavailable and no residues result; the interim use of chemical processes should be reviewed.

Requires competent authorities to establish inspection and certification systems (aligned with international guidelines) operated by designated authorities and/or officially recognized bodies and to oversee approval, supervision and delegation arrangements; assessment criteria for recognition include documented procedures, penalties, resources and objectivity, while authorities must audit, verify inspections, require access and reports, protect confidential information, and apply remedies (lot removal of organic indications or marketing bans) and information exchange when irregularities are found.

Permits imports of specified organic products only when an exporting‑country competent authority issues an original certificate attesting the lot was produced, prepared, marketed and inspected in accordance with these guidelines and any equivalency decision; the original certificate must accompany the first consignee and transactional records kept for at least two years, organic status must be maintained through distribution (quarantine treatments not conforming to these guidelines revoke organic status), and importing countries may require equivalency documentation, site visits, or specific labelling in line with their Section 3 provisions.
Annex 1 heading identifying that what follows are the principles of organic production (heading only, no substantive provisions in this entry).

For plant and plant products, sets conversion requirements (minimum two years before sowing, three years for perennials unless competent authority adjusts but at least 12 months), requires conversion to begin only once under inspection and following production rules, allows progressive conversion with holdings split into units, prohibits alternating between organic and conventional production, mandates soil fertility management (rotations, organic matter, compost, limited Annex 2 inputs only when necessary), and prescribes ecological and cultural pest/disease/weed controls with Annex 2 products only for imminent serious threats.
Fragment/heading introducing pest, disease and weed control measures (list start) and not a substantive standalone section on livestock and livestock products.

Specifies general conversion principles: minimum conversion periods (two years, three for perennials, with minimum 12 months in exceptional adjustments), conversion may only start once the production unit is under the inspection system and implementing Section 4 rules, progressive/partial conversion is allowed with holdings split into units, and areas must not be alternated between organic and conventional methods.
Allows progressive conversion of parts of a farm using permitted techniques and requires that where a whole farm is not converted at once the holding be split into units as specified in Annex 3.

Sets conversion rules: minimum conversion periods (two years, three for perennials), conversion begins only after the unit is placed under the inspection system and implements Section 4 production rules, progressive conversion is permitted and areas must not be alternated between organic and conventional production.

Requires maintaining or increasing soil fertility through multi‑annual rotation with legumes/green manures/deep‑rooting plants and by incorporating organic material or compost from holdings producing in accordance with the guidelines, with livestock by‑products permitted only if they originate from compliant holdings.

States that substances listed in Annex 2, Table 1 may be applied only when adequate crop nutrition or soil conditioning cannot be achieved by the rotation and organic matter measures or, in the case of manures, when such manures are not available from organic farming.

Permits use of substances listed in Annex 2 Table 1 only as a last resort for crop nutrition or soil conditioning when methods in 5(a) and (b) cannot achieve adequate nutrition, or where manures are not available from organic farming.
Lists permitted measures including release of natural enemies (predators/parasites), biodynamic preparations from stone meal, farmyard manure or plants, and mulching and mowing.
Identifies manure management practices to include mulching and mowing and grazing of animals.
Includes grazing of animals and mechanical controls (traps, barriers, light and sound) as listed measures.
Allows mechanical controls (traps, barriers, light and sound) and permits steam sterilization where proper soil renewal rotation cannot be implemented for species-specific requirements.

sections.section: ANNEX 2.                                       PERMITTED SUBSTANCES FOR THE PRODUCTION OF ORGANIC FOODS
sections.summary: Annex title/TOC entry for 'Permitted Substances for the Production of Organic Foods' and cross-reference to Annex 3; not substantive text.

sections.section: ANNEX 3.                                       MINIMUM INSPECTION REQUIREMENTS AND PRECAUTIONARY MEASURES UNDER THE INSPECTION OR CERTIFICATION SYSTEM
sections.summary: Table of contents entry for Annex 3 on minimum inspection requirements and precautionary measures, showing parts A (production units), B and C (preparation/packaging units and imports); not substantive.
TOC entry 'A.' referencing Production units in Annex 3; not substantive.
TOC entry 'B. C.' referencing preparation and packaging units and imports in Annex 3; not substantive.

sections.section: ## GUIDELINES FOR THE PRODUCTION, PROCESSING, LABELLING AND MARKETING OF ORGANICALLY PRODUCED FOODS
sections.summary: Document title heading 'Guidelines for the Production, Processing, Labelling and Marketing of Organically Produced Foods' with code GL 32-1999; not substantive.

Foreword stating the guidelines' purpose to protect consumers and producers, harmonize inspection, certification and labelling for organic foods and facilitate import equivalence, while emphasizing organic principles (minimize external inputs, sustain soil, biodiversity and welfare), requiring inspection/certification separation and regular review and updating of the guidelines.

Defines scope: these guidelines apply to unprocessed plants/plant products, livestock and processed agricultural products carrying organic-production labelling, specify when terms like 'organic' imply production method, and exclude materials/products from genetically engineered/modified organisms.
Section heading for 'Description and Definitions'; not substantive on its own.

States foods may be described as organic only if produced in farm systems that nurture sustainable agroecosystems (crop rotation, residue recycling, water/tillage management, biological/cultural/mechanical pest control) that optimize soil biological activity and fertility, and that livestock husbandry promotes welfare, minimizes stress and avoids chemical allopathic veterinary drugs including antibiotics.

Provides definitions for terms used in the guidelines including agricultural product, audit, certification and certification body, competent authority, a provisional definition of genetically engineered/modified organisms and listed techniques, ingredient, inspection, labelling, livestock, marketing, official accreditation/recognition, operator, plant protection product, preparation, production and veterinary drug.
Section heading for 'Labelling and Claims'; not substantive on its own.

Sets labelling requirements: organic products must comply with the Codex General Standard and may claim organic only if production/import, inspection and certification conditions (referencing Sections 4, 6 and 7) are met and the label names the official/recognized inspection or certification body; for processed products all agricultural ingredients must be from organic sources (with certain permitted exceptions), prohibited treatments/substances are barred, and member countries may adopt specific rules/derogations (e.g., up to 5% non‑organic agricultural ingredients or percentage‑based labelling such as 95%/70% with front‑panel percentage declarations and ingredient-list formatting).

sections.section: Labelling of products in transition/conversion to organic
sections.summary: Allows 'transition to organic' labelling for farm products after 12 months of organic production provided labelling/inspection requirements are met, the wording is not misleading and is less prominent than the product description, single-ingredient foods may show it on the principal display panel, and the label must reference the official/approved certification body.
Requires labelling of non-retail containers of products in Section 1.1 to comply with the requirements set out in Annex 3, paragraph 10.

sections.section: 5.2 Countries should develop or adopt a list of substances that meet the criteria outlined in Section 5.1.
sections.summary: Obliges countries to develop or adopt lists of substances that meet the criteria specified in Section 5.1.

Section 6 sets requirements for inspection and certification systems for organic foods, requiring competent authorities to establish, approve and supervise designated and/or officially recognized inspection/certification bodies (with possible delegation to a non-inspecting 'designate'), specifies approval criteria (procedures, penalties, resources, objectivity), audit, reporting and confidentiality duties, and mandates removal of organic indications, marketing prohibitions for serious infringements, and application of international information‑exchange guidance on rejections.

sections.section: 6.1 Inspection and certification systems are used to verify the labelling of, and claims for, organically produced foods.
sections.summary: Inspection and certification systems must be used to verify labelling and organic claims and their development should follow international principles/guidelines (e.g., CAC/GL 20-1995, CAC/GL 26-1997, ISO65).

sections.section: 6.2 Competent authorities should establish an inspection system operated by one or more designated authorities and/or officially recognized inspection/certification bodies to which the operators producing, preparing or importing products as referred to in paragraph 1.1 should be subject.
sections.summary: Competent authorities must establish an inspection system operated by one or more designated authorities and/or officially recognized inspection/certification bodies to which producers, preparers and importers of organic products (per paragraph 1.1) are subject.

sections.section: 6.3 The officially recognized inspection and certification systems should comprise at least the application of the measures and other precautions set out in Annex 3.
sections.summary: Officially recognized inspection and certification systems must at minimum apply the inspection measures and precautions listed in Annex 3.

sections.section: 6.4 For the application of the inspection system operated by the official or officially recognized certification body or authority, countries should identify a competent authority responsible for the approval and supervision of such bodies:
sections.summary: Countries must identify a competent authority responsible for approving and supervising certification/inspection bodies, allowing delegation of assessment/supervision to a non‑inspecting designate while retaining responsibility, and permitting an importing country to recognize a third‑party accreditor if the exporting country lacks an identified competent authority and national program.

sections.section: 6.5 In order to attain approval as an officially recognized certification body or authority, the competent authority, or its designate, when making its assessment should take into account the following:
sections.summary: To approve a certification/inspection body the competent authority (or its designate) must assess the body's standard inspection/certification procedures and measures, intended penalties for irregularities, availability of qualified staff and facilities, inspection experience/reliability, and the body's objectivity toward inspected operators, noting that inspection and certification roles must be clearly separated if performed by the same body.

sections.section: 3. 6.6 The competent authority or its designate should:
sections.summary: The competent authority or its designate must ensure inspections are objective, verify their effectiveness, record irregularities and penalties, and withdraw approval of a certification body that fails to meet objectivity, effectiveness or the approval criteria (including paragraphs 6.7–6.9).

sections.section: 8. 6.7 Official and/or officially recognized certification bodies or authority referred to in paragraph 6.2 should:
sections.summary: Officially recognized certification bodies must apply at least the inspection measures in Annex 3 to inspected undertakings and must not disclose confidential inspection/certification information except to the responsible operator and competent authorities.

sections.section: 2. 6.8 Official or officially recognized inspection and/or certification bodies or authority should:
sections.summary: Official or officially recognized inspection/certification bodies must grant the competent authority or its designate access to offices, facilities and operators' sites for audits and provide annually a list of operators inspected and a concise annual report.

sections.section: 5. 6.9 The designated authority and the official or officially recognized certification body  or authority referred to in paragraph 6.2 should:
sections.summary: The designated authority and certification body must remove organic indications from any entire lot affected by an irregularity and, for manifest or prolonged infringements, prohibit the operator from marketing products with organic indications for a period agreed with the competent authority or its designate.

sections.section: 8. 6.10 The  requirements  of  the Guidelines  for  the  Exchange  of  Information  between  Countries  on  Rejections  of Imported Food 19 should  apply  where  the  competent  authority  finds  irregularities  and/or  infringements  in  the application of these guidelines.
sections.summary: Where the competent authority finds irregularities or infringements of these guidelines it must apply the Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejections of Imported Food (CAC/GL 25-1997).

Imported products may be marketed only with an exporting‑country competent authority certificate confirming the lot was produced/prepared/inspected under systems meeting these guidelines and equivalency decisions; the original certificate must accompany the goods and importers must retain transactional certificates for at least two years, maintain product authenticity through to the consumer (quarantine treatments incompatible with the guidelines void organic status), and importing countries may require equivalency information, expert reports, site visits and labeling in accordance with import country requirements.
Non‑substantive heading indicating the start of Annex 1.
Non‑substantive heading titled ‘Principles of Organic Production’.

For plant production this Annex requires a conversion period (minimum two years before sowing, three years for perennial crops before first harvest, adjustable by the competent authority but not below 12 months), placement under the inspection system before conversion begins, prohibition on alternating fields between organic and conventional production, maintenance/improvement of soil fertility by crop rotations and organic amendments (with Annex 2 substances only where necessary), and pest/weed control primarily by species/variety choice, rotations, mechanical and biological methods (with steam sterilization and Annex 2 products allowed only for imminent or serious threats).
